verses.text: Now the Philistines gathered together all their armies to Aphek: and the Israelites pitched by a fountain which is in Jezreel. And the lords of the Philistines passed on by hundreds, and by thousands: but David and his men passed on in the rearward with Achish. Guidance: The chronology of the chapter overlaps with chapter 28; we are watching the same Philistine muster from a different angle. The full Philistine confederation has gathered at Aphek (the old battlefield where the ark was lost in chapter 4 — a deliberate echo). The Israelites are at the spring in Jezreel, downhill from Gilboa where Saul's army camped (28:4). The Philistine units pass by in formation — the standard ancient muster, with infantry in hundreds and thousands organized by city — and at the very end of the column come 'David and his men… in the rearward with Achish.' David's position is the king's bodyguard's position — directly behind and protecting Achish himself. Six hundred Israelite men, marching at the back of the Philistine army, headed to fight Israel. The visual is the worst-case picture of where chapter 27's compromise has led. David is moments away from being committed to combat against his own people. He has not yet been able to extricate himself; he is going through the motions. The chapter is going to show how the LORD pulls him out — not by David's wisdom but by Philistine suspicion.

Now the Philistines gathered together all their armies to Aphek: and the Israelites pitched by a fountain which is in Jezreel. And the lords of the Philistines passed on by hundreds, and by thousands: but David and his men passed on in the rearward with Achish. Guidance: The chronology of the chapter overlaps with chapter 28; we are watching the same Philistine muster from a different angle. The full Philistine confederation has gathered at Aphek (the old battlefield where the ark was lost in chapter 4 — a deliberate echo). The Israelites are at the spring in Jezreel, downhill from Gilboa where Saul's army camped (28:4). The Philistine units pass by in formation — the standard ancient muster, with infantry in hundreds and thousands organized by city — and at the very end of the column come 'David and his men… in the rearward with Achish.' David's position is the king's bodyguard's position — directly behind and protecting Achish himself. Six hundred Israelite men, marching at the back of the Philistine army, headed to fight Israel. The visual is the worst-case picture of where chapter 27's compromise has led. David is moments away from being committed to combat against his own people. He has not yet been able to extricate himself; he is going through the motions. The chapter is going to show how the LORD pulls him out — not by David's wisdom but by Philistine suspicion.

Then said the princes of the Philistines, What do these Hebrews here? And Achish said unto the princes of the Philistines, Is not this David, the servant of Saul the king of Israel, which hath been with me these days, or these years, and I have found no fault in him since he fell unto me unto this day? Guidance: The other Philistine commanders see the Israelite contingent and immediately object. 'What do these Hebrews here?' is suspicious, not friendly. The four other Philistine seranim — the city-lords of Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, and Ekron, who together with Achish of Gath form the council of five — do not share Achish's confidence in David. Achish defends: 'Is not this David, the servant of Saul the king of Israel?' Achish is still operating on the chapter-27 theory: David is Saul's defector, my asset, fully vetted. 'I have found no fault in him since he fell unto me unto this day' — the verb 'fell' (naphal) is interesting; it can mean 'deserted' but also has overtones of dropping in lap, almost an unexpected gift. Achish thinks David has been gift-wrapped to him by Israel's civil war. His confidence is, in the immediate sense, completely justified by David's behavior. But the other four are sharper. The five-against-one structure of the Philistine council is going to save David where his own caution had failed. Sometimes the LORD's rescue arrives through the prudent suspicion of one's enemies' colleagues.

And the princes of the Philistines were wroth with him; and they said unto him, Make this fellow return, that he may go again to his place which thou hast appointed him, and let him not go down with us to battle, lest in the battle he be an adversary to us: for wherewith should he reconcile himself unto his master? should it not be with the heads of these men? Is not this David, of whom they sang one to another in dances, saying, Saul slew his thousands, and David his ten thousands? Guidance: The Philistine commanders are furious and give Achish two arguments. (1) Military: 'lest in the battle he be an adversary to us.' They fear David will turn coat at the decisive moment — switch sides mid-battle and become 'an adversary' (satan, the word for adversary) inside their own lines. Their reasoning is sound: 'wherewith should he reconcile himself unto his master? should it not be with the heads of these men?' What better way for David to reconcile with Saul than to deliver to him the heads of Philistine officers? It is, in fact, precisely the kind of move David could have made — and the commanders are wise enough to see it. (2) Political: 'Is not this David, of whom they sang… Saul his thousands, and David his ten thousands?' The Israelite song that drove Saul into jealousy is the same song known to Philistine intelligence. They have not forgotten that David's reputation was built specifically on killing Philistines. The very song Saul read as a threat the Philistines read as a credential — an anti-credential, from their perspective. The man is too dangerous to have at our backs. The decision is final: send him home. The Philistine refusal of David's service is, theologically, God's rescue of David's character. God uses the very Philistine memory of David's reputation to prevent David from doing the thing that would have erased it.

Then Achish called David, and said unto him, Surely, as the LORD liveth, thou hast been upright, and thy going out and thy coming in with me in the host is good in my sight: for I have not found evil in thee since the day of thy coming unto me unto this day: nevertheless the lords favour thee not. Wherefore now return, and go in peace, that thou displease not the lords of the Philistines. Guidance: Achish breaks the news gently. His oath formula is striking: 'as the LORD liveth' — a Philistine king swearing in the name of Israel's God, probably as a diplomatic courtesy to the Israelite he is dismissing. Whether Achish himself believes in the LORD or is simply using the name David would honor most, the effect is the same: the chapter has Achish testifying to David's uprightness in the LORD's own name. The narrator's irony is rich. Achish thinks David has been upright; the reader knows David has been spending sixteen months lying about his raid targets. The compliment Achish pays David is one David does not in fact deserve — and the LORD is using Achish's mistaken belief in David's integrity as the very mechanism of rescuing David's actual integrity. 'Go in peace, that thou displease not the lords of the Philistines.' David is to leave at first light. The marching order is reversed by sundown: David came north as Achish's bodyguard; he goes south as a discharged ally. The career arc — Israelite hero, Philistine bodyguard, dismissed ally — has reached its strangest point and is about to reverse. God has just rescued David from his worst-case moral fate without David doing anything to deserve the rescue.

'Go in peace, that thou displease not the lords of the Philistines.' David is to leave at first light. And David said unto Achish, But what have I done? and what hast thou found in thy servant so long as I have been with thee unto this day, that I may not go fight against the enemies of my lord the king? Guidance: David's protest is one of the great masterworks of double-meaning in the Old Testament. 'But what have I done?' Achish hears it as a wounded loyalist objecting to dismissal; the reader hears it as a man whose conscience is silent. 'What hast thou found in thy servant?' The reader knows there is plenty to be found, all of which Achish has missed. The line that does the heaviest work is the last: 'that I may not go fight against the enemies of my lord the king.' Whose 'lord the king'? Achish naturally hears 'me'; David's words, parsed literally, never specify. 'My lord the king' could be Achish or could be Saul — and in fact Saul, the LORD's anointed king of Israel, would by David's own theology still be the 'lord the king' to whom David owes ultimate allegiance. David is uttering a line so ambiguous that he can preserve his loyalty to Israel while sounding loyal to Achish. The whole chapter has David buying time and saying things that mean different things to different ears. The performance is so good that even now, with the exit ramp opened by God, David puts up a show of disappointment. The chapter ends without David ever quite confessing what he is doing; God simply pulls him out anyway. There is a category of God's mercy in which He extricates us from situations we never quite stopped pretending we wanted to stay in.

And Achish answered and said to David, I know that thou art good in my sight, as an angel of God: notwithstanding the princes of the Philistines have said, He shall not go up with us to the battle. Guidance: Achish's compliment is again ironic: 'thou art good in my sight, as an angel of God.' A Philistine king pays an Israelite vassal the supreme religious compliment — 'as an angel of God' (kemalakh elohim). The chapter is showing us a man whose ability to evaluate David is comically inverted; he literally cannot see the duplicity in front of him. The narrator does not mock Achish; he simply records what Achish sees. But the reader understands: Achish has been so persuaded by David's performance that he now describes David in language reserved for the most sacred beings in the LORD's economy. God uses Achish's flat-out wrong evaluation of David's character as the gracious cover for David's exit. 'Notwithstanding the princes of the Philistines have said, He shall not go up with us to the battle.' Achish has been overruled by his colleagues. He has the title of king but not the unilateral power to keep David on the battle roster. The Philistine pentapolis was a confederation; the four other seranim outvoted Achish. The political constraint Achish frames as a frustration is exactly the rescue God needed it to be. God's mercies often arrive through the politics of one's adversaries' internal disputes.

Wherefore now rise up early in the morning with thy master's servants that are come with thee: and as soon as ye be up early in the morning, and have light, depart. So David and his men rose up early to depart in the morning, to return into the land of the Philistines. And the Philistines went up to Jezreel. Guidance: The marching orders. 'Rise up early in the morning… as soon as ye be up early in the morning, and have light, depart.' The triple emphasis on dawn is Achish's signal that there must be no delay — the other Philistine commanders are watching, and any lingering would be interpreted as conspiracy. David and his men must be on the road south at first light. They obey. They travel back to their land — Ziklag. Meanwhile, the Philistine main force marches north to Jezreel. The chapter splits the two armies geographically by sentence's end: David southbound; Philistines northbound; Saul on Gilboa. The next chapter (30) will follow David to Ziklag — where he will arrive to find his town burned and his family taken captive by Amalekite raiders. The chapter's rescue is real but partial; the LORD has extracted David from the Philistine battle, but the consequences of the sixteen-month compromise are not yet fully reckoned. David is about to find that walking out of one disaster does not always mean walking into peace. The geography of the day is the geography of God's hand on history. While the Philistines march to victory on Gilboa and Saul to his death, David is rerouted back to Ziklag — where his next crisis is already burning. The chapter is one of the cleanest pictures in scripture of how God rescues a believer from a self-built trap, but does not always exempt them from learning what the trap cost.
